This chapter, and many that follow, repeat the genealogies, or lists of fathers and children in the Bible history, and put them together with many additions. When compared with other passages, some differences are found; yet we must not therefore be troubled by this, but bless God that the things necessary for salvation are plain enough. The origin of the Jewish nation is here traced from the first man that God created, and is thereby distinguished from the obscure, fabulous, and absurd origins assigned to other nations. But now the nations are all so mixed with one another that no one nation, nor the greatest part of any, is descended entirely from any of these sources. We are only sure that God has created all nations of men from one lineage; they are all descended from one Adam, one Noah. Do we not all have one father? Has not one God created us?

The genealogy is now limited to the descendants of Abraham. Let us take occasion from reading these lists of names to think of the multitudes that have passed through this world, have done their parts in it, and then left it. As one generation, even of sinful people, passes away, another comes, and will continue to do so while the earth remains. Our time on earth is short as we move into eternity. May we be recognized as the Lord’s people.
